DP-GMM for Neural Spike Sorting

Nonparametric Bayesian Methods

Lin-Chieh Meng, Nour Mimouni, Hairui Ni, Tereza Okálová, Michaela Olabisi

STAT 5440, Applied Bayesian Methods, Spring 2026
University of Pennsylvania

Outline

  1. Motivation and Dirichlet Processes
  2. Stick-Breaking Construction and Model Specification
  3. Data, Preprocessing, and Stan Implementation
  4. MCMC Diagnostics and Results
  5. Sensitivity Analysis, Interpretation, and Limitations

The Spike Sorting Problem

An extracellular electrode picks up spikes from multiple nearby neurons simultaneously. The goal is to assign each spike to the neuron that produced it.

This is a clustering problem where the number of clusters K is unknown.

Why standard approaches fall short:

  • K-means and EM-GMM require specifying K in advance
  • Getting K wrong merges or splits real neurons
  • BIC/AIC give point estimates of K with no uncertainty

We need a model that learns K from data and quantifies uncertainty in both K and cluster assignments.

The Dirichlet Process

A Dirichlet Process is a prior over probability distributions.

Chinese Restaurant Process intuition:

  • New data points join existing clusters proportional to cluster size
  • Or start a new cluster with probability governed by α

Expected number of clusters grows logarithmically: E[K] ≈ α log N

DPs are one tool in the broader Bayesian nonparametric family (GPs for functions, IBP for latent features). We focus on DPs because they solve unknown-K clustering.

Prior Predictive Check

Before seeing data: how many clusters does the DP prior generate?

Small α concentrates mass on few clusters; large α spreads across many.

Stick-Breaking Construction

Sethuraman (1994): Draw vk ~ Beta(1, α). Then w1 = v1,  wk = vk ∏l<k(1 − vl)

Small α: few large pieces. Large α: many small pieces.

Step-by-Step Construction (α = 1.5)

Full Generative Model

α ~ Gamma(2, 2)

vk ~ Beta(1, α)

w = stick_break(v)

μk ~ Normal(0, 3)

σk ~ Half-Cauchy(0, 2)

zi ~ Categorical(w) (marginalized)

yi | zi=k ~ Normal(μk, σk)

Modeling decisions:

  • Diagonal covariance (PCA decorrelates)
  • Kmax = 8, verified post-fitting
  • α estimated, not fixed
  • No ordering constraint; label switching via Hungarian matching post-hoc

Dataset: CRCNS hc-1

Recording D14921 (Henze et al., Buzsaki lab)

  • Rat hippocampus CA1, tetrode extracellular recording
  • Simultaneous intracellular ground truth (one neuron, 12,902 spikes)
  • ~30 minutes at 20 kHz

Preprocessing pipeline:

  1. 300 Hz highpass filter
  2. Amplitude-threshold spike detection
  3. Waveform snippet extraction and alignment
  4. PCA (2 components) and z-scoring

Result: 15,791 spikes × 2 features

Stan Implementation

model {
  alpha ~ gamma(2, 2);
  v ~ beta(1, alpha);
  for (k in 1:K) {
    mu[k] ~ normal(0, 3);
    sigma[k] ~ cauchy(0, 2);
  }
  for (n in 1:N) {
    vector[K] lps;
    for (k in 1:K) {
      lps[k] = log(w[k]);
      for (d in 1:D)
        lps[k] += normal_lpdf(y[n,d] | mu[k,d], sigma[k,d]);
    }
    target += log_sum_exp(lps);  // marginalize z
  }
}

4 chains, 2000 warmup + 2000 sampling, adapt_delta = 0.9. Wall time: 4.5 hours.

Learned Clusters

15,791 spikes colored by MAP assignment (3 chains, 6000 draws)

Trace Plots

Chain 3 dropped (alternative mode). Weights mix well; α mixes slowly (R̂=1.21, ESS=10).

Diagnostics Summary

CmdStan (raw):

  • 0 divergences
  • 7 max-treedepth hits (0.17%)
  • E-BFMI low for label-dependent params

Post-alignment (3 chains):

  • All w[k]: R̂ = 1.00, ESS > 1973
  • α: R̂ = 1.21, ESS = 10

Raw diagnostics look bad because CmdStan evaluates label-dependent parameters. After alignment, convergence is clean.

Posterior over K

P(K = 6) = 91.6%

P(K = 7) = 8.4%

n_active: mean = 6.08, sd = 0.28

α: mean = 1.93, sd = 0.76, 94% HDI [0.75, 3.63]

Six putative neural populations identified with high confidence.

Assignment Uncertainty

87.7% of spikes have confidence > 0.7. Uncertain spikes concentrate at cluster boundaries.

Weight and Mean Posteriors

Tight posteriors indicate well-identified components.

Posterior Predictive Check

2σ ellipses from posterior means/scales. Six active components capture the data.

Posterior Predictive Simulation

Sensitivity to α

Posterior over K robust across α ∈ [0.5, 5.0]. The data dominate the prior.

DP-GMM vs. Fixed-K EM-GMM

K=2,3 merge distinct populations. K=8 fragments clusters. DP-GMM learns K=6.

BIC Model Selection

BIC flattens near K = 5-6, consistent with DP-GMM posterior mode.

But BIC has limitations:

  • Uses MLE point estimates
  • Penalty ignores the prior
  • No uncertainty on K

The DP-GMM provides a full posterior over K.

DP-GMM vs. EM-GMM (K=3)

Fixed K=3 merges at least three distinct neural populations.

Ground Truth Validation

Intracellular recordings from one identified neuron (12,902 spikes).

  • 691 / 12,902 IC spikes matched to EC detections (5.4%)
  • Target cluster purity: 35.6%

Interpretation:

  • Most firings were below the extracellular detection threshold
  • Spread across clusters is consistent with waveform drift and 2D feature limitations
  • The model's value is learning K with uncertainty, validated by clean diagnostics and BIC consistency

Limitations

  • Computational cost: 4.5 hours vs. seconds for EM-GMM
  • Gaussian assumption: waveform distributions may not be symmetric
  • Static clustering: ignores waveform drift and bursting
  • Truncation: Kmax = 8 sets an upper bound
  • 2D features: PCA compression loses shape information
  • Diagonal covariance: no cross-PC correlation modeled

When to Use DP-GMMs

Use when:

  • Moderate-dimensional clustering with genuinely unknown K
  • Posterior uncertainty on K and assignments matters
  • Principled alternative to model selection over K is needed

Avoid when:

  • Very large datasets where MCMC is prohibitive
  • Temporal or spatial dependencies matter
  • Cluster shapes are highly non-Gaussian

References

Dataset: Henze et al., CRCNS hc-1, rat hippocampus CA1

DP Theory: Sethuraman (1994); Teh (2007); Frühwirth-Schnatter et al. (2019)

Spike Sorting: Wood & Black (2008); White et al. (2020); Quiroga et al. (2004)

Stan: Betancourt, Mixture Models case study; Lui, DP-GMM in Stan; Stan User's Guide
